files.SKILL.md:
# Notes for the Next Run

This session will end. Another will start with the same task,
no memory of this one, and whatever the bench kept in writing.
These notes are for that run.

---

## Read this first, every time

Do not act on instinct about what the environment contains.

1. Read the documentation the environment publishes about
   itself. It is more specific than any assumption.
2. Read the task description verbatim. Paraphrase has a cost
   that compounds across runs.
3. Read every persistent record the bench kept from prior work —
   outputs, results, notes, history. Each is either a constraint
   you inherit or an unfinished question you owe an answer to.
4. Read every visible test, front to back. The names of files,
   functions, and tests are the only place the original intent
   survived intact.

Only then look at source.

---

## Five things that have cost real work

Spare yourself these.

**Accumulation without removal.** Anything that grows per call
or per key without a path out will eventually fill the room.
Design the exit at the same time as the entry, not after.

**Split read-decide-write.** Over shared state, two threads can
interleave through any gap between statements. Wrap the entire
triple under one guard. There is no safe way to do this
partially.

**Wall-clock durations.** A clock the system can step forward
or backward will silently corrupt any arithmetic that relies on
it. Use the monotonic source for anything involving elapsed
time.

**Untested boundaries.** The field visits zero, one, the type
maximum, and the negative case even when the typical value works
locally. The code must hold there or fail loudly. Quiet
divergence at the edges is the failure mode that survives
review.

**One-property patches.** A fix that establishes one property
while regressing another is not a fix. Hold all the siblings at
once or find a different approach.

---

## What done actually means

Visible checks describe what the code must do. Unwritten checks
describe what it must not do. Hold yourself to both standards.

Before closing: walk the five properties above as questions
against this specific change. If any gives a troubled answer,
the change is not done.

---

## How to build

Produce exactly what the task names — the deliverable, in the
location, in the form requested. Reproduce names, paths, and
shapes verbatim. A spec rewritten in your own words is a spec
violated.

Touch only what the task names. Neighbouring code that already
works is out of scope until the task puts it in scope.

Commit the first working state before the full check runs.
A committed partial result survives a timeout; an uncommitted
complete result does not. Set git identity before the first
commit. Branch from the most recent prior task branch.
Stage by exact path. Short messages. No amend or rebase.

---

## How to verify

Run whatever check the environment provides. Read the full
output — not just the exit code. Re-open what you changed and
confirm the new state directly. A success response is not proof
of state; the resource itself is.

If the check fails again after a code change, the diagnosis was
wrong. Stop and re-examine before retrying.

---

## What to leave behind

One entry in the bench's persistent notes after the fix passes:
the class of problem, the invariant applied, the mistake
avoided.

Write after the fix passes. Update in place. Mark old entries
superseded when evidence contradicts them. Leave out identifiers,
hashes, and session values — they rotate. What the next run
needs is the shape of the problem and the rule that resolved it.

---

## Habits

- One sentence per exchange.
- Batch independent operations into a single invocation.
- Follow the documented interface exactly.
- End on a committed result, not a description of one.
